title: Personal Finance: a field manual
text:
Welcome!
Welcome to the Personal Finance field manual, a working reference for the day-to-day decisions that shape your financial life: budgeting, savings, borrowing, investing, taxes, insurance, and planning.
This book aims to answer questions like:

title: Personal Finance: a field manual
text:
How this book is organized
The book is divided into five parts:

Foundations – budgeting, tracking spending, emergency funds, and debt.
Savings – savings strategies, high-yield accounts, and automation.
Investing – the basics, retirement accounts, index funds, and asset allocation.
Taxes & insurance – the protective layer around your finances.
Planning – goals, major purchases, and estate planning.

The five sections of the book map onto the four levers:

Foundations sets up income and spending

Savings turns the gap between them into a buffer

Investing puts that buffer to work

Taxes & insurance and Planning sit around the outside

Each chapter is short and self-contained so you can jump in where you need to.

title: Personal Finance: a field manual
text:
Code
If you’d like to follow along or perform any of the math yourself, the examples use both R (with the tidyverse) and Python (with pandas / NumPy). Pick whichever you prefer – the underlying ideas are the same.


show/hide
1000 * (1 + 0.05)^10  # future value of $1,000 at 5% for 10 years
#> [1] 1628.895




show/hide
1000 * (1 + 0.05) ** 10
#> 1628.894626777442

title: Personal Finance: a field manual
text:
About the author
Martin Frigaard writes about R, data, and – now – personal finance.

title: Foundations
text:
The four levers
Personal finance is mostly behavior and a small amount of math. The math matters – compounding, interest rates, tax-advantaged accounts – but most of your personal finances depends on your habits. These three questions will determine how much money you’ll have at any point in your life:
Before you optimize investments or chase tax savings, the foundations need to be in place: a working budget, awareness of where money goes, a buffer against bad surprises, and a plan for any high-interest debt.
This chapter sets the stage for the rest of the book:
Each lever has its own part in this book:

title: Foundations
text:
Income and spending are covered in Foundations.
Savings – the gap between the two – has its own part: Savings.
Investing is what happens to savings over the long term: Investing.

title: Foundations
text:
Time horizon
Money you need in 12 months should not look like money you won’t touch for 30 years. The rest of the book builds on that distinction.
Once the foundations are in place, the next lever is Savings: turning the gap between income and spending into a deliberate, recurring habit.

title: 1  Income
text:
1.1 Some Core Truths
Building wealth doesn’t start with picking the right stock or finding a hot investment—it starts with how you manage the money flowing in and out of your life. Let’s break down the foundational principles.
Here’s a counterintuitive insight from Morgan Housel:
The person driving the flashy car isn’t necessarily wealthy—they just spent their money on a car. True wealth is the income you didn’t spend, quietly compounding in the background.
Ben Felix reinforces this with hard data: research consistently shows that conspicuous consumption (signaling wealth through purchases) is negatively correlated with actual wealth.2
People who look rich usually aren’t. People who are rich typically live below what their income would allow.

title: 1  Income
text: Wealth is the nice cars not purchased. The diamonds not bought. The watches not worn, the clothes forgone and the first-class upgrade declined. Wealth is financial assets that haven’t yet been converted into the stuff you see.1

title: 1  Income
text:
1.2 Your Human Capital
One of Felix’s most powerful contributions is reframing how we think about income itself. He encourages investors to view themselves as having two forms of capital. When you’re young, your single biggest asset is your future earning potential.






%%{init: {'theme': 'neutral', 'look': 'handDrawn', 'themeVariables': { 'fontFamily': 'monospace', "fontSize":"16px"}}}%%

flowchart TD
    TotalWealth(["Your Total<br>Economic Wealth"]) --> HumanCapital("Human Capital:<br/>Future Earning Potential")
    TotalWealth --> FinancialCapital("Financial Capital:<br/>Investments & Savings")
    HumanCapital --> EarlyCareer("Early Career:<br/>Human Capital is HUGE")
    FinancialCapital --> EarlyCareer2("Early Career:<br/>Financial Capital is small")
    EarlyCareer --> Implication["Implication:<br>Invest in Skills,<br/>Education &<br>Career Growth"]
    EarlyCareer2 --> Implication2[Implication:<br>Take More Equity<br>Risk When Young]



 Human Capital 



Investing in skills, education, and career mobility often has a higher return than any portfolio decision. Income growth, especially early in your career, is one of the most powerful wealth-building levers available.

title: 1  Income
text:
1.3 Spending and Happiness
The academic research on money and well-being tend to show some surprisingly practical conclusions:

Experiences beat possessions. Research shows experiential purchases generate more lasting happiness than material ones.
Buying time is one of the highest-return uses of money. Outsourcing tasks you dislike (cleaning, lawn care, commuting time) correlates strongly with life satisfaction.
Lifestyle inflation is the silent killer. Hedonic adaptation means each lifestyle upgrade quickly becomes the new baseline—but the financial commitment remains permanent.
Housing is the biggest lever. Because shelter is most people’s largest expense, decisions about how much house to buy or rent have an outsized impact on lifetime wealth.Renting is often financially equivalent or superior to buying when the math is done honestly.

title: 1  Income
text:
1.4 Three Anchoring Rules

1. Spend on What You Love, Cut on What You Don’t (Sethi)
Love coffee? Buy the $6 latte. But maybe drive a reliable used car instead of a luxury lease. The goal isn’t deprivation—it’s intentionality.


2. Build a Gap Between Ego and Income (Housel)
The wider the gap between what you earn and what your ego demands you spend, the wealthier you become. Lifestyle creep is the silent killer of financial freedom.


3. Keep It Simple (Bogle)
Bogle’s philosophy applies to spending too: complexity is the enemy. The more complicated your financial life, the easier it is to lose track. Automate, simplify, repeat.

title: 1  Income
text:
1.5 As Your Income Grows
This is where most people fail. A raise feels like permission to upgrade everything. Instead, focus on Felix’s research-backed advice: the marginal happiness gained from lifestyle upgrades is much smaller than people expect, while the marginal financial security from saving raises is enormous.





%%{init: {'theme': 'neutral', 'themeVariables': { 'fontFamily': 'monospace', "fontSize":"16px"}}}%%

flowchart TD
    Raise(["Pay Raise"]) --> Split{"<strong>Split the Raise</strong>"}
    Split --> HalfInvest["50% to<br>Investing/Savings"]
    Split --> HalfLifestyle["50% to<br>Lifestyle"]
    HalfInvest --> WealthGrows(["Wealth Compounds"])
    HalfLifestyle --> EnjoyNow(["Enjoy the Win"])


 Interactive debugger with browser() or breakpoint 



This way you reward yourself and accelerate freedom—without falling into the trap of inflating your lifestyle to match every dollar.

title: 1  Income
text:
1.6 The Bottom Line
Living within your means isn’t about restriction—it’s about control and intention. As Housel puts it, the highest form of wealth is the ability to wake up and say, “I can do whatever I want today.” Felix would add that this control comes from a combination of growing your income, protecting your savings rate, and being evidence-based about what actually makes you happy.
Start here:
1. Calculate your savings rate (savings + investments ÷ take-home pay)
2. Invest in your human capital—your career is your biggest asset early on
3. Automate transfers on payday so you never see the money
4. Track your fixed costs—aim to keep them under 60% of take-home
5. Spend the rest without guilt on what genuinely brings you joy—prioritizing experiences and time over possessions

title: 1  Income
text:
From The Psychology of Money↩︎
Read more here↩︎

title: 2  Budgeting
text:
2.1 What this chapter covers
A budget is a plan for the money you expect to receive. The goal isn’t to restrict yourself – it’s to make spending intentional.

title: 2  Budgeting
text:
Fixed vs. variable expenses
Zero-based vs. percentage budgets
Common budgeting tools and spreadsheets

title: 2  Budgeting
text: 2.2 What is a budget?

title: 2  Budgeting
text:
2.3 Savings Rate
Your savings rate matters more than your income or investment returns, especially early on. Someone earning $60,000 who saves 20% will often outpace someone earning $120,000 who saves 5%.





%%{init: {'theme': 'neutral', 'look': 'handDrawn', 'themeVariables': { 'fontFamily': 'monospace', "fontSize":"16px"}}}%%

flowchart LR
    Income(["Monthly Income"]) --> Taxes["Taxes"]
    Income --> FixedCosts["Fixed Costs<br/>50-60%"]
    Income --> Investments["Investments<br/>10%+"]
    Income --> SavingsGoals["Savings Goals<br/>5-10%"]
    Income --> GuiltFreeSpend["Guilt-Free Spending<br/>20-35%"]
    
    FixedCosts --> Rent("Rent/Mortgage")
    FixedCosts --> Utilities("Utilities & Insurance")
    FixedCosts --> Groceries("Groceries & Transport")
    FixedCosts --> MinDebt("Minimum Debt<br>Payments")


 Conscious Spending Plan 



This is Ramit Sethi’s Conscious Spending Plan—a flexible framework rather than a rigid budget.

title: 2  Budgeting
text:
2.4 The Four Buckets
The table below displays the ‘four buckets’ or categories your income should be split into.








Bucket
Target %
Purpose




Fixed Costs
50–60%
Rent, utilities, groceries, transport, minimum debt payments


Investments
10%+
401(k), Roth IRA, index funds (pay your future self first)


Savings Goals
5–10%
Emergency fund, vacations, down payment, gifts


Guilt-Free Spending
20–35%
Dining out, hobbies, clothes—enjoy life now

title: 2  Budgeting
text:
2.5 Living Within Your Means

“Spending money to show people how much money you have is the fastest way to have less money.” — Morgan Housel, The Psychology of Money






%%{init: {'theme': 'neutral', 'look': 'handDrawn', 'themeVariables': { 'fontFamily': 'monospace', "fontSize":"16px"}}}%%

flowchart TD
    Start(["Take-Home Pay"]) --> PayYourselfFirst{"<strong>Automate First</strong>"}
    PayYourselfFirst --> InvestAuto["Auto-transfer<br>to Investments"]
    PayYourselfFirst --> SaveAuto["Auto-transfer<br>to Savings"]
    InvestAuto --> Remainder("What's Left =<br> Your Spending Limit")
    SaveAuto --> Remainder
    Remainder --> SpendFreely(["Spend Freely<br>Within This<br>Amount"])


 Automate to keep it simple 



The trick: Don’t budget what’s left after spending. Spend what’s left after saving and investing. This single mental shift changes everything.

title: 2  Budgeting
text:
2.6 A simple starting framework: 50 / 30 / 20

50% needs (housing, food, utilities, minimum debt payments)
30% wants (dining out, entertainment, travel)
20% savings & extra debt repayment



show/hide
income <- 5000
data.frame(
  Bucket = c("Needs (50%)", "Wants (30%)", "Save/Debt (20%)"),
  Amount = c(income * 0.50, income * 0.30, income * 0.20)
)



  





show/hide
income = 5000
{
    "Needs":    income * 0.50,
    "Wants":    income * 0.30,
    "Save/Debt": income * 0.20,
}


{'Needs': 2500.0, 'Wants': 1500.0, 'Save/Debt': 1000.0}

title: 3  Tracking Spending
text:
You only manage what you measure. This chapter covers how to capture every dollar that leaves your accounts and turn that data into insight.

Categorizing transactions
Reviewing on a monthly cadence
Spotting “leak” categories (subscriptions, delivery fees, impulse buys)

title: 4  Emergency Fund
text:
An emergency fund is cash set aside to handle the unexpected without reaching for credit. The standard target is 3 to 6 months of essential expenses, parked somewhere safe and liquid.

How much you actually need
Where to keep it (and where not to)
How to rebuild it after a withdrawal

title: 5  Managing Debt
text:
Not all debt is equal. A 3% mortgage and a 24% credit card balance call for very different strategies.

Good debt vs. bad debt
The avalanche method (highest rate first) vs. the snowball method (smallest balance first)
Refinancing and consolidation
When to prioritize debt payoff over investing

title: Savings
text:
Savings are the bridge between earning and investing – the third of the four levers introduced in the Introduction. This part covers how much to save, where to keep it, and how to make the whole process automatic so willpower isn’t part of the equation.
A practical sequence:

Build a starter buffer (covered in Emergency Fund).
Pick a target savings rate (Savings Strategies).
Park the money where it actually grows (High-Yield Savings Accounts).
Automate the whole flow (Automating Savings).

From there, the money is ready to feed into Investing.

title: 6  Savings Strategies
text:
How much should you save? The honest answer is: as much as you can sustain. This chapter covers common targets and how to choose one that matches your goals.

Pay-yourself-first
Sinking funds for known future expenses
The savings rate as the single most important number

title: 7  High-Yield Savings Accounts
text:
If your cash is sitting in a 0.01% account at a big bank, you’re leaving real money on the table. A high-yield savings account (HYSA) at an online bank or via a money-market fund can pay many multiples more.

What to look for in an HYSA
FDIC / NCUA coverage
Money market funds as an alternative
When CDs and T-bills make sense

title: 8  Automating Savings
text:
The single most effective trick in personal finance: make saving the default, not a decision. If money leaves your checking account automatically on payday, you’ll adapt your spending to what’s left.

Direct deposit splits
Automatic transfers and the “two-account” setup
Increasing contributions on a schedule

title: Investing
text:
Savings keep your purchasing power intact; investing grows it. This part covers the core ideas – compounding, diversification, low-cost index funds, and the right account types to hold them in.
Investing is the fourth and final lever from the Introduction. It assumes the previous levers are working: income is coming in, spending is under control, and savings are accumulating somewhere they can be moved into the market.
The aim is a portfolio you can leave alone for decades.

title: 9  Investing Basics
text:
9.1 Compounding
A short tour of the concepts behind every investment decision.
Compounding is interest earning interest. Over long horizons, it does the heavy lifting.
show/hide
years   <- 0:40
balance <- 10000 * (1.07)^years
plot(years, balance, type = "l",
     xlab = "Years", ylab = "Balance ($)",
     main = "$10,000 at 7% compounded annually")
show/hide
years = list(range(0, 41))
balance = [10000 * (1.07) ** y for y in years]
balance[-1]  # ending balance after 40 years


149744.57839206984

title: 9  Investing Basics
text:
9.2 Risk vs. return
Higher expected return = higher expected volatility. Choose a level you can stick with through downturns, not just one that looks attractive on a spreadsheet.

title: 9  Investing Basics
text:
9.3 Diversification
Don’t bet on one company, one sector, or one country. Broad index funds make this nearly free.

title: 10  Retirement Accounts
text:
Retirement accounts are tax-advantaged wrappers that hold your investments. Choosing the right one is often worth more than picking the right fund.

401(k) / 403(b) / TSP – employer plans, often with a match
Traditional IRA – tax-deferred contributions
Roth IRA – tax-free growth
HSA – the most tax-advantaged account available if you qualify
Taxable brokerage – for everything above the limits

A good default order: capture the employer match, max the HSA (if eligible), max IRA, then back to the 401(k) up to the limit.

title: 11  Index Funds
text:
A low-cost, broad-market index fund is the closest thing personal finance has to a free lunch. You own a tiny slice of thousands of companies, pay almost nothing in fees, and beat most active managers over long periods (Bogle 2017).

Expense ratios – why a 0.04% vs. 1% fee matters
Total-market vs. S&P 500 vs. international
ETFs vs. mutual funds





Bogle, John C. 2017. The Little Book of Common Sense Investing. 10th Anniversary. Wiley.

title: 12  Asset Allocation
text:
Asset allocation is how you divide your portfolio between stocks, bonds, and cash. It’s the single biggest driver of long-term results – bigger than fund selection or market timing.

Stocks vs. bonds vs. cash
Glide paths and target-date funds
Rebalancing
Why a three-fund portfolio is enough for most people (Collins 2016)





Collins, J. L. 2016. The Simple Path to Wealth. CreateSpace.

title: Taxes & Insurance
text: Taxes and insurance are the protective layer around the rest of your finances. Get them roughly right and the rest is much easier. Get them wrong and a single event can undo years of saving.

title: 13  Taxes
text:
You don’t need to be a tax expert – you need to know enough to avoid obvious mistakes and capture obvious wins.

Marginal vs. effective tax rates
Pre-tax vs. Roth contributions
Tax-loss harvesting in taxable accounts
Common deductions and credits
When to hire a CPA

title: 14  Insurance
text:
Insurance is for losses you can’t absorb on your own. Buy enough to cover the catastrophic; skip the small policies that turn premiums into a slow leak.

Health
Auto
Renters / homeowners
Term life (when you have dependents)
Disability
Umbrella

title: Planning
text: Long-term planning takes the building blocks from the earlier parts of this book and turns them into specific, dated goals: a down payment, a sabbatical, retirement, what happens to your assets after you’re gone.

title: 15  Financial Goals
text:
A “goal” without a number and a date is a wish. This chapter is about turning wishes into plans you can actually execute against.

Short / medium / long horizons
Sequencing goals when you can’t fund them all at once
Tracking progress without obsessing over it

title: 16  Major Purchases
text:
Houses, cars, and weddings are where most people accidentally undo years of saving. The mechanics aren’t complicated – the discipline is.

Down payment math
Total cost of ownership (the sticker price is the smallest piece)
Financing vs. paying cash
Avoiding lifestyle creep after a raise

title: 17  Estate Planning
text:
You don’t need a complicated trust to do the basics right. A will, named beneficiaries on every account, and a power of attorney cover most people.

Will and (sometimes) trust
Beneficiary designations override your will – keep them current
Power of attorney and healthcare directives
Where to keep the paperwork so it can actually be found

title: Resources
text:
Books
A short, opinionated list of further reading and tools.

text:

The Little Book of Common Sense Investing – John C. Bogle (Bogle 2017)

text:
Communities

r/personalfinance – the wiki is unusually good

title: Resources
text:
Tools

Spreadsheets (Google Sheets / Excel) – still the most flexible option
Account aggregators (Monarch, Copilot, YNAB)
